title: Authentication - Overview - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Authentication
extractedEntities.description: The process by which Foundry validates user identity through one or more identity providers, supporting SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect 1.0 (OIDC) protocols

extractedEntities.name: Identity Provider
extractedEntities.description: External system that validates users and provides Foundry with information about users, attributes, and groups for authentication and access management

extractedEntities.name: SAML 2.0
extractedEntities.description: Security Assertion Markup Language protocol supported by Foundry for identity provider integration and single sign-on

extractedEntities.name: OpenID Connect
extractedEntities.description: OIDC 1.0 protocol supported by Foundry for identity provider integration alongside SAML 2.0

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform that manages access through identity provider integrations

extractedEntities.name: Control Panel
extractedEntities.description: Foundry administration interface used to set up and manage identity provider integrations via the Authentication tab

extractedEntities.name: User Attributes
extractedEntities.description: Structured information about users (name, email, job title) provided by identity providers to Foundry for access management

extractedEntities.name: Groups
extractedEntities.description: Collections of users provided by identity providers that Foundry uses for access management and role assignment

keyInsights: Foundry authentication is fully delegated to external identity providers, allowing organizations to reuse their existing IdP infrastructure
Two standard protocols are supported for IdP integration: SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect 1.0 (OIDC)
Identity providers supply not just authentication but also user metadata including attributes and group memberships
The Control Panel Authentication tab is the central management interface for all identity provider integrations

title: Checkpoints - Overview - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoints
extractedEntities.description: Data governance tool that interrupts potentially sensitive user interactions with configurable prompts requiring justification, supporting purpose justification and auditability across 60+ Foundry interactions

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoint Configuration
extractedEntities.description: Settings that define the prompt content, justification type required, and which users see a checkpoint for specific interaction types

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoint Record
extractedEntities.description: Audit record containing timestamp, user identity, justification provided, checkpoint type, and associated data (resources, objects, Markings) from a governed interaction

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform into which Checkpoints is deeply integrated across 60+ different interaction types

extractedEntities.name: Purpose Justification
extractedEntities.description: The requirement for users to explain why they are performing a sensitive action, with justifications recorded for audit review

extractedEntities.name: Approvals
extractedEntities.description: Foundry feature that works alongside Checkpoints for asynchronous interaction requests

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Security classifications associated with data that appear in checkpoint records as contextual information

extractedEntities.name: Organization
extractedEntities.description: Foundry organizational unit within which checkpoint configurations and records can be managed

keyInsights: Checkpoints are integrated into 60+ different interactions in Foundry, providing comprehensive coverage of sensitive user workflows
Checkpoint records capture rich contextual data including timestamp, user, justification, and associated resources/Markings for complete audit trails
Checkpoints support both synchronous (user-initiated) and asynchronous (via Approvals) interaction governance
Configuration is highly granular: administrators can control who sees checkpoints, for which interaction types, with custom prompt language and justification requirements
The Checkpoints application serves dual purpose: configuration management for administrators and personal justification review for all users

title: Cipher - Core concepts - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Cipher
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's encryption and hashing service that provides column-level data encryption through Channels and Licenses, supporting both probabilistic and deterministic encryption modes

extractedEntities.name: Cipher Channel
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry filesystem resource that defines a specific protocol for obfuscating or de-obfuscating values, including encryption algorithm, key parameters, or hashing algorithm and secret

extractedEntities.name: Cipher License
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry filesystem resource that controls permissions to use cryptographic operations defined in a parent Cipher Channel, granting access to users who can view the License

extractedEntities.name: Cipher Encrypted Value
extractedEntities.description: Encrypted value format following the syntax CIPHER::<channel-rid>::<encrypted-value>::CIPHER, embedding metadata needed for decryption

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform that hosts Cipher as a security service within the filesystem workspace

extractedEntities.name: Deterministic Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Encryption mode where the same plaintext always produces the same ciphertext, preserving join capability across encrypted columns

extractedEntities.name: Probabilistic Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Encryption mode where the same plaintext produces different ciphertexts each time, providing stronger security but preventing joins on encrypted values

keyInsights: Cipher provides two distinct encryption modes: deterministic (preserves join capability) and probabilistic (stronger security), allowing administrators to trade off between data utility and protection level
The Channel-License model separates encryption configuration from access control: Channels define the cryptographic protocol while Licenses control who can use those operations
Cipher encrypted values are self-describing with the format CIPHER::<channel-rid>::<encrypted-value>::CIPHER, embedding enough metadata for the service to decrypt without external context
Licenses are standard Foundry filesystem resources that can be moved, shared, and permissioned like any other resource, leveraging existing access control infrastructure

title: Security glossary - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Mandatory Access Controls
extractedEntities.description: All-or-nothing access restrictions in Foundry implemented through Markings and Organizations that cannot be overridden by discretionary grants

extractedEntities.name: Discretionary Access Controls
extractedEntities.description: Additive capabilities granted via Roles that expand what a user can do beyond their base access, cannot restrict permissions

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory access control mechanism representing a data classification; when applied to a resource, users without the marking are guaranteed to never access that data

extractedEntities.name: Organization
extractedEntities.description: A mandatory access control boundary that determines which users can access resources; resources can require membership in one or more organizations

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Sets of operations granting specific capabilities on resources. Four default roles exist: Owner, Editor, Viewer, and Discoverer

extractedEntities.name: Access Requirements
extractedEntities.description: The combination of Organizations and Markings that determine which users may access a Project or resource

extractedEntities.name: Groups
extractedEntities.description: Collections of users and/or other groups that can be internal (Foundry-defined) or external (from identity providers)

extractedEntities.name: Identity Provider
extractedEntities.description: System that validates users during login and provides information on users, groups, and attributes

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform with a dual security model combining mandatory and discretionary access controls

extractedEntities.name: Expand Access
extractedEntities.description: Any change in access requirements that broadens the potential audience, such as removing a Marking or adding Organizations when at least one is already applied

keyInsights: Foundry security uses a dual model: Mandatory controls (Markings + Organizations) determine IF a user can access a resource, while Discretionary controls (Roles) determine WHAT they can do with it
Discretionary controls are strictly additive -- they can only grant additional permissions, never restrict them, preventing accidental permission removal through role changes
Four default platform Roles exist (Owner, Editor, Viewer, Discoverer) that can be customized or extended by role administrators
Groups can be internal (Foundry-defined) or external (from identity providers like Active Directory), and internal groups can contain external groups, enabling hybrid access management
Access expansion events are tracked carefully: removing Markings always expands access, while Organization changes follow specific rules depending on whether organizations are already applied

title: How Palantir Meets IL6 Security Requirements with Apollo - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's continuous delivery and deployment management platform that enables patching, updating, and managing services across environments including air-gapped and classified networks

extractedEntities.name: IL6 Authorization
extractedEntities.description: DISA Impact Level 6 provisional authorization covering potentially classified data, the highest currently defined impact level for cloud services in the DoD

extractedEntities.name: Cryptographic Signing
extractedEntities.description: Artifact integrity validation mechanism where all Apollo artifacts are cryptographically signed to ensure they have not been tampered with

extractedEntities.name: Vulnerability Management
extractedEntities.description: Apollo's capability to detect vulnerabilities, alert teams, and use severity criteria to gate deployments, preventing vulnerable code from reaching production

extractedEntities.name: Air-Gapped Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Deployment pattern for disconnected networks where Apollo manages software delivery without continuous internet connectivity

extractedEntities.name: FedRAMP
extractedEntities.description: Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program providing standardized security assessment for cloud service providers

extractedEntities.name: NIST Risk Management Framework
extractedEntities.description: Framework used by the DoD for security evaluations, providing guidance for identifying and assessing security controls in IT systems

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform that operates within IL6-authorized environments deployed and managed by Apollo

keyInsights: Apollo achieves 3.5-minute average patch/update deployment time, reducing production issue remediation from hours to under 5 minutes
Palantir received DISA IL6 provisional authorization in October 2022, the highest impact level covering potentially classified data
Apollo's design supports disconnected and air-gapped networks, critical for defense and intelligence environments where continuous internet connectivity is not available
All deployment artifacts are cryptographically signed for integrity validation, and vulnerability detection gates prevent deploying compromised code
IL6 is based on DISA's Cloud Computing SRG and NIST RMF, creating a standardized 'Do Once, Use Many' accreditation approach for government cloud services

title: Security - Authorization - Palantir Apollo Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's deployment management platform using role-based access controls (RBAC) for authorization across resources like Release Channels, Products, and Environments

extractedEntities.name: RBAC
extractedEntities.description: Role-Based Access Control model used by Apollo where Teams gain permissions to operations based on assigned roles, grantable globally per resource type or per specific resource instance

extractedEntities.name: Apollo Roles
extractedEntities.description: Named permission sets in Apollo including Administrator, Contributor, Restricted Contributor, Viewer, Approver, and resource-specific role variants

extractedEntities.name: Release Channel
extractedEntities.description: Apollo resource representing a deployment channel (e.g., STAGING) with roles including Administrator, Contributor, Restricted Contributor, and Viewer

extractedEntities.name: Teams
extractedEntities.description: Groups of users in Apollo that are assigned roles, granting all team members the operations that the role permits

extractedEntities.name: Vulnerability Suppression Roles
extractedEntities.description: Apollo role set for vulnerability management including Administrator, Approver, and Viewer roles for suppression settings

extractedEntities.name: Product Roles
extractedEntities.description: Apollo role set for product management including Product Administrator, Release Creator, Product Creator, Product Editor, and Product Viewer

extractedEntities.name: Environment Roles
extractedEntities.description: Apollo role set for environment management including Administrator, Upgrader, and Viewer roles

keyInsights: Apollo RBAC supports both global grants (all resources of a type) and specific resource grants (e.g., only the STAGING Release Channel), providing flexible permission scoping
Roles are assigned to Teams rather than individual users, ensuring consistent permission management across groups
Release Channels have a nuanced contributor model: Contributor can add any Product Release, while Restricted Contributor can only add releases they own
Apollo separates configuration and secrets from infrastructure, enabling secure reapplication onto new systems without exposing sensitive deployment data
Each resource type in Apollo has its own role hierarchy: Release Channels, Products, Labels, Environments, and Vulnerability settings all have distinct role sets

title: Object permissioning - Managing object security - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Object Security Policy
extractedEntities.description: Ontology-level policy governing visibility of object instances, enabling row-level security independent of backing data source permissions

extractedEntities.name: Property Security Policy
extractedEntities.description: Ontology-level policy governing visibility of individual property values, enabling column-level security independent of backing data source permissions

extractedEntities.name: Cell-Level Security
extractedEntities.description: Combined row and column level security in the Ontology achieved through object and property security policies working together

extractedEntities.name: Data Source Policy
extractedEntities.description: Legacy security approach where object type permissions are implicitly controlled by the permissions applied to the input data sources

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity (e.g., Passenger) whose instances and properties can be secured at row, column, and cell levels through security policies

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attribute of an Object Type (e.g., Name, Address, Phone Number) that can be individually secured via property security policies

extractedEntities.name: Row-Level Security
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism restricting visibility of entire object instances (rows) based on markings or other access controls

extractedEntities.name: Column-Level Security
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism restricting visibility of specific properties (columns) based on markings like PII

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool where object and property security policies can be directly configured on each object type

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory access control labels (e.g., VIP, PII) applied to objects or properties to enforce row-level and column-level security

keyInsights: Object and property security policies are the recommended approach over data source policies, providing unified cell-level security directly on the object type rather than requiring complex multi-datasource configurations
Cell-level security is the combination of row-level (object instances) and column-level (properties) security, achieved through separate but complementary policy types
Object security policies are independent of backing data source permissions, decoupling Ontology access control from data infrastructure permissions

Media reference properties create a potential security gap: property visibility and referenced media set visibility are independently controlled, requiring administrators to align permissions across both
The Passenger example illustrates the canonical pattern: VIP marking for row-level security on certain passengers, PII marking for column-level security on Name/Address/Phone Number properties

title: Ontology Manager - Ontology roles migration - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Roles
extractedEntities.description: Authorization model for Ontology resources (object types, link types, action types) that decouples metadata permissions from data permissions, replacing legacy datasource-derived permissions

extractedEntities.name: Datasource-Derived Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Legacy authorization model where Ontology resource permissions were implicitly derived from the permissions on input data sources

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource that can be permissioned via Ontology roles, with metadata permissions decoupled from data instance permissions

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource that can be permissioned via Ontology roles alongside object types and action types

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource that can be permissioned via Ontology roles alongside object types and link types

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Editor Role
extractedEntities.description: Role granting permission to edit Ontology resources and their metadata without requiring permissions on the underlying data source

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Owner Role
extractedEntities.description: Role with full control over an Ontology resource including the ability to manage permissions on that resource

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool where Ontology role metadata permissions are managed and migration from legacy permissions is performed

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology feature requiring Ontology roles to be enabled, part of the newer Ontology capabilities

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology feature requiring Ontology roles to be enabled, allowing property type definitions to be shared across object types

keyInsights: Ontology roles fundamentally decouple metadata permissions (who can edit the Object Type definition) from data permissions (who can see individual object instances), which remain governed by data source permissions
Ontology roles became the default authorization model for all new enrollments since September 2023, replacing datasource-derived permissions
Migration to Ontology roles is required to use newer Ontology features including shared property types and interfaces

After migration, all users with access to the Ontology gain view permission on all migrated resources (object types, link types, action types), making resource metadata universally visible within the Ontology
Migration can be performed incrementally -- there is no required ordering, and mixed permission models (some resources on old model, some on new) are supported during transition
The latest evolution moves permissioning to the Compass filesystem, making Ontology roles themselves a legacy approach being superseded

title: Functions - Language agnostic features - Permissions - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Ontology computation unit whose authoring and execution are subject to multiple permission layers including repository, Ontology entity, and caller permissions

extractedEntities.name: Function Execution Context
extractedEntities.description: Permission context under which a function runs, determining what data it can access based on caller identity and repository permissions

extractedEntities.name: Repository Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Project-level permissions granted to a functions repository that determine which Ontology entities and data sources are accessible during code generation and execution

extractedEntities.name: Caller Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Permissions of the user invoking a function, which constrain what data the function can access during execution

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity that must be explicitly imported into a repository's Project for function code generation and data access

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity that must be explicitly imported alongside object types for complete function code generation

extractedEntities.name: Code Assist
extractedEntities.description: Foundry infrastructure for live preview function execution, subject to the same permission requirements as code generation in repositories

extractedEntities.name: Restricted View
extractedEntities.description: Special data source import required for object types with row-level security enabled, used instead of direct dataset imports

extractedEntities.name: Function Binding
extractedEntities.description: Auto-generated code bindings for every object and link type loaded based on repository permissions during code generation

keyInsights: Functions operate under a three-layer permission model: repository permissions (what entities are available), Ontology entity permissions (what types are accessible), and caller permissions (who is running the function)
Repository permissions are distinct from user permissions and must be explicitly configured -- a user's personal access does not automatically transfer to their repository
Ontology entities (object types, link types) must be explicitly imported at the Project level, and changes in one repository's imports affect all repositories in the same Project
Published functions enforce caller permissions at execution time, ensuring the function can only access data the invoking user is authorized to see
Object types with row-level security require importing Restricted Views instead of direct datasets, adding an additional security layer for sensitive data in function contexts

title: Trust in Data (Palantir Explained #4) - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Data Provenance
extractedEntities.description: Tracking pattern that traces data from its original source through all transformations, enabling users to assess reliability and make informed decisions about data quality

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: End-to-end visibility into how data flows and transforms through the platform, from raw ingestion to final analytical outputs

extractedEntities.name: Audit Trail
extractedEntities.description: Comprehensive record of data operations and transformations that enables accountability and transparency in data-driven decision making

extractedEntities.name: Data Quality Management
extractedEntities.description: Systematic approach to monitoring, validating, and ensuring completeness, accuracy, and timeliness of data across an organization

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform providing comprehensive data provenance, lineage, and quality management capabilities

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Foundry entity whose data quality and provenance can be traced through the platform's lineage tracking capabilities

extractedEntities.name: COVID-19 Response
extractedEntities.description: Real-world use case where data trust, accountability, and transparency were critical for pandemic response decision-making

extractedEntities.name: Data Harmonization
extractedEntities.description: Process of integrating data from multiple heterogeneous sources into a unified, standardized format for analysis

keyInsights: Data trust in Foundry is built on three pillars: provenance (where data came from), lineage (how it was transformed), and audit trails (who did what)
Poor data quality can propagate insidiously through an organization -- a single bad dataset can compromise entire data-driven initiatives
Data quality must be paired with transparency: users with relevant roles and permissions should have full context necessary to assess and trust their data
Foundry addresses the fundamental challenge of harmonizing data from heterogeneous sources with differing standards, definitions, schemas, and formats into a single source of truth
The COVID-19 response demonstrated that data trust tenets of accountability and transparency are especially critical in crisis situations

title: Getting started - Protecting sensitive data - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Foundry mandatory access controls representing a data classification (e.g., PII, PHI) that propagate along data lineage and guarantee non-members can never access marked data

extractedEntities.name: Marking Category
extractedEntities.description: Named grouping of related markings (e.g., 'Information' category containing PII and PHI markings) for organized classification management

extractedEntities.name: Marking Propagation
extractedEntities.description: Behavior where markings automatically flow downstream along data lineage, ensuring derived data inherits the security classifications of its sources

extractedEntities.name: Marking Simulation
extractedEntities.description: Feature allowing administrators to preview the impact of applying markings to a pipeline before committing, showing which downstream datasets will be affected

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform providing comprehensive sensitive data protection through markings, organizations, restricted views, Cipher, and Checkpoints

extractedEntities.name: Restricted View
extractedEntities.description: Security feature that provides a filtered view of data, removing sensitive columns while maintaining downstream accessibility

extractedEntities.name: Cipher
extractedEntities.description: Foundry encryption service used as part of the comprehensive data protection strategy for sensitive data types

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoints
extractedEntities.description: Governance tool used alongside markings for purpose justification when accessing sensitive data

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Object Type attribute (e.g., date of birth) that may contain sensitive PII data requiring marking-based protection

extractedEntities.name: Sensitive Data Protection Strategy
extractedEntities.description: Combined pattern using markings, organizations, restricted views, Cipher, and Checkpoints to build comprehensive data protection for PII and PHI

keyInsights: Markings propagate automatically along data lineage, meaning applying a PII marking to a raw dataset will cascade to all downstream derived datasets
Marking simulation mode allows administrators to preview the full lineage impact of applying a marking before committing, preventing accidental lockouts of downstream users
The recommended strategy is to keep sensitive data in the pipeline as long as possible and remove sensitive columns only at the final Ontology output layer

Five complementary tools form the comprehensive data protection strategy: Markings (mandatory access), Organizations (boundary controls), Restricted Views (filtered access), Cipher (encryption), and Checkpoints (justification)
Marking categories provide organizational structure (e.g., 'Information' category) enabling scalable classification as new data types (PII, PHI, etc.) are identified

title: Management - Manage roles - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Named sets of operations in Foundry that grant specific capabilities on resources; four default roles (Owner, Editor, Viewer, Discoverer) can be customized or extended

extractedEntities.name: Operations
extractedEntities.description: Individual permissions checked by Foundry applications to verify a user can perform a given action, identified by unique identifiers like 'stemma:mutate-default-branch'

extractedEntities.name: Role Sets
extractedEntities.description: Groups of roles applied at the Organization level that allow customization of role permissions in specific contexts like Projects or the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: Custom Roles
extractedEntities.description: User-defined roles that can include other roles and be granted additional operations, such as Merger or Supporter roles for specialized workflows

extractedEntities.name: Default Roles
extractedEntities.description: Four built-in Foundry roles: Owner (full control), Editor (modify), Viewer (read), and Discoverer (limited visibility)

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform with customizable role-based access control at the Organization level

extractedEntities.name: Operation Identifier
extractedEntities.description: Unique string identifying a Foundry permission, following the format namespace:action-name (e.g., 'stemma:mutate-default-branch')

extractedEntities.name: Merger Role
extractedEntities.description: Example custom role providing ability to merge to protected branches, combining Viewer permissions with additional merge-related operations

extractedEntities.name: Supporter Role
extractedEntities.description: Example custom role for support teams who can see Project issues but not metadata like dataset schemas, designed for third-party support with restricted data visibility

keyInsights: Roles are composed of Operations, which are individual permission checks identified by unique namespaced identifiers (e.g., 'stemma:mutate-default-branch')
Custom roles can include other roles as a base, enabling role composition -- the Merger role includes all Viewer operations plus additional merge-specific operations
Default roles can only be edited through custom role sets, not directly, protecting the baseline permission model while allowing Organization-level customization

Role sets enable Organization-level customization of permissions for different contexts (Projects, Ontology), allowing different organizations on the same enrollment to have different role definitions
The Supporter role pattern demonstrates how to give third-party support teams issue visibility without exposing dataset metadata, schemas, or other sensitive operational details

title: Use case patterns - Multi-organization ecosystems - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Multi-Organization Ecosystem
extractedEntities.description: Architectural pattern where multiple organizations coexist within a Foundry enrollment with strict data isolation while enabling controlled cross-organizational data and application sharing

extractedEntities.name: Organization
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory access control boundary in Foundry that determines user access to resources; each organization fully owns its own space and data governance

extractedEntities.name: Market Ecosystem
extractedEntities.description: Ecosystem model where one organization introduces an ecosystem platform and other organizations participate as vendors, app developers, or consumers

extractedEntities.name: Cross-Organization Data Sharing
extractedEntities.description: Pattern enabling organizations within an ecosystem to securely share data in standardized formats while maintaining complete control over what is shared and what remains private

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform enabling cross-organizational ecosystems with proven delivery across aviation, automotive, finance, and pharmaceuticals industries

extractedEntities.name: Data Isolation
extractedEntities.description: Guarantee that organizations within a shared enrollment maintain strict separation of data unless explicitly shared through controlled mechanisms

extractedEntities.name: Supply Chain Ecosystem
extractedEntities.description: Use case where retailers, manufacturers, and vendors share inventory and demand data within an ecosystem for mutual operational benefit

extractedEntities.name: Government Inter-Agency Collaboration
extractedEntities.description: Use case where government agencies share data within a Foundry ecosystem while maintaining agency-level data governance and security boundaries

extractedEntities.name: Guest Access
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism allowing users from one organization to access specific resources in another organization within the same ecosystem

keyInsights: Foundry's multi-organization model enables ecosystem-level collaboration while maintaining strict data isolation -- each party controls their own space and governance with precise control over sharing
Organizations move beyond a central point of control: Foundry divides permissions such that every party independently manages their own data governance
Multiple ecosystem models are supported and often combined as the ecosystem grows, including Market Ecosystem and peer-to-peer sharing patterns
The pattern has proven delivery across multiple industries: aviation, automotive, finance, and pharmaceuticals, demonstrating cross-domain applicability
The fundamental value proposition is converting siloed organizations into collaborative ecosystems, reducing lost resources, accelerating innovation, and preventing repeated mistakes

title: Compute modules - Configuration - Execution modes - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Compute Module
extractedEntities.description: Foundry resource for hosting custom logic in any language that can operate in Function mode (serving logic across Foundry) or Pipeline mode (strict provenance-controlled data processing)

extractedEntities.name: Function Execution Mode
extractedEntities.description: Compute module mode where custom logic is registered as functions callable from Workshop, Developer Console, or OSDK, with optional platform permissions via service user

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Execution Mode
extractedEntities.description: Compute module mode enforcing strict provenance control where all inputs/outputs pass through the Foundry build system with job tokens scoped to specific resources

extractedEntities.name: Zero Trust Security Model
extractedEntities.description: Security approach applied to compute modules where no implicit trust is granted and all access must be explicitly authorized through scoped tokens or permissions

extractedEntities.name: Job Token
extractedEntities.description: Scoped access token attached to pipeline-mode compute modules, limited to specifically declared input and output resources

extractedEntities.name: Application Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Function mode permission model where a service user determines platform API and OSDK access, consistent regardless of the invoking user

extractedEntities.name: No Platform Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Function mode permission model where the compute module has no access to platform APIs or OSDK, only running isolated logic with optional external egress

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application platform that can invoke compute module functions in function execution mode

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology SDK that can invoke compute module functions and is accessible via application permissions in function mode

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application providing provenance tracking for pipeline-mode compute modules, tracing data flow across resources

extractedEntities.name: OAuth Token Exchange
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism for compute modules with application permissions to exchange client ID and secret for an access token with desired permissions

keyInsights: Compute modules support two fundamentally different security postures: Function mode (serving logic to applications) and Pipeline mode (strict provenance-controlled data processing)
Pipeline mode enforces data provenance by requiring all inputs/outputs to flow through the Foundry build system, enabling complete lineage tracking via the Data Lineage application
Function mode offers a permission spectrum: No Platform Permissions (fully isolated) vs Application Permissions (service-user-based platform access), allowing precise control over module capabilities
Application permissions use a dedicated service user whose access is consistent regardless of who invokes the function, providing predictable and auditable access patterns
The zero trust model means compute modules receive no implicit access -- pipeline mode uses scoped job tokens while function mode requires explicit permission configuration

title: Third-party applications - Foundry third-party application & API guidance - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: OAuth2 Authorization Code Flow
extractedEntities.description: Authentication mechanism allowing external applications to act on behalf of individual Foundry users with explicit consent, ensuring correct permissions and clear audit trail

extractedEntities.name: Third-Party Application
extractedEntities.description: Non-Foundry application or script that interacts with Foundry APIs through OAuth2 authentication, subject to scope restrictions and appropriate use policies

extractedEntities.name: Scope Restrictions
extractedEntities.description: Limitations on what operations a third-party application can perform through Foundry APIs, enforced through OAuth2 token scoping

extractedEntities.name: Token Isolation
extractedEntities.description: Security requirement that data accessed with one user's token must never be exposed to another user, preventing circumvention of Foundry's authorization model

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform providing OAuth2-based third-party application integration with strict data isolation and scope restrictions

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Ontology capability accessible to third-party applications through Foundry APIs for executing logic and interacting with the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: Mobile Ontology Application
extractedEntities.description: Example appropriate use: mobile phone application interacting with Foundry's Ontology and Actions APIs for streamlined critical workflows

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Monitoring Application
extractedEntities.description: Example appropriate use: application connecting to Foundry's monitoring and data health APIs to assess pipeline state and trigger builds

extractedEntities.name: Service User Migration
extractedEntities.description: Recommended pattern of replacing service account-based integrations with OAuth2 Authorization Code Flow for proper per-user permissions and audit trails

keyInsights: OAuth2 Authorization Code Flow is the recommended authentication mechanism for third-party applications, replacing service accounts to ensure per-user permissions and clear audit trails

Token isolation is a critical security requirement: data accessed with one user's Foundry token must never be exposed to another user, preventing authorization bypass through the third-party application layer
Inappropriate API use includes circumventing data controls (cross-user token data sharing), performing actions without user consent, and building competing data management interfaces outside Foundry
The guidance explicitly calls out that third-party application integration presents risks to data security and should only be undertaken with system administrator approval

Appropriate uses include external system integration, custom mobile workflows against the Ontology, and pipeline monitoring -- all patterns that extend Foundry's reach while maintaining its security model
